title: Today's AI Brief: So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that
summary: A rundown of the latest AI news, from decoding AI terms to Braintrust's use of Codex.
imageAlt: Image from source article for So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that
imageCaption: So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that
imageSourceTitle: So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that

body.text: Artificial intelligence is changing the world, and with it, a whole new language is being invented. I am looking at a piece from TechCrunch that dives into the definitions of common AI terms. You know, the ones we've all nodded along to in meetings - LLMs, RAG, RLHF, and more. It's time to decode them.

First up, TechCrunch has a great guide to common AI terms. I've always wondered what 'hallucinations' meant in AI - turns out it's when an AI generates false information. Not exactly what I do when I'm sleepy, but I digress. This guide is a great starting point for anyone looking to get up to speed on AI lingo.

Next, OpenAI has a post on how Braintrust uses Codex with GPT-5.5 to turn customer requests into code. What stands out to me is how Braintrust engineers can run experiments and code faster. It's like having a super-smart intern who's really good at coding - but without the coffee breaks.

Another interesting bit from the world of AI is the advancements in how machines are being taught. Apparently, some of these AI systems are being fine-tuned with a technique called Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, or RLHF. The idea is to get AI to behave more like humans, or at least, not completely unlike us.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
